title: Scroll-scene beat configuration
description: Declare beats before modelling anything. Beats are the storyboard; the scene is built to serve them, never the reverse.

journey.description: The continuous environmental gradient across the whole scroll. Must be monotonic — cold to warm, high to low, open to intimate. This single gradient is what makes scrolling feel like a journey rather than a carousel.

motion.description: Springs, never lerp. Damping is brand.

fov.description: MUST be the lowest stiffness of the three. The lens lagging the body under acceleration is the cinematic tell.

scrub.description: Scroll lag. ~1.8 reads premium; 0 reads mechanical.

beats.description: Under 6 the scroll feels thin. Over 9 viewers stop reading and start scrubbing.

transitions.description: One per adjacent beat pair. Storyboard the middle or it gets improvised at code time, where changing it is expensive.

mechanic.description: Exactly one. Two mechanics at once cancel and read as a glitch.

still.description: Path to the approved midpoint storyboard frame.

strategy.description: Never a throttled canvas. A WebGL scene on a phone drains battery, janks under the exact gesture the design depends on, and looks broken rather than simple.

frames.description: One composed 9:16 still per beat. Portrait framing chosen for portrait — not a 16:9 crop.

subject.description: What the subject is doing here. Must match a named animation state.

idea.description: MANDATORY, one sentence: the single thing the viewer must understand at this beat. A beat that cannot state its idea is a camera position, not a beat — cut it. This field removes more bloat than any other rule in the system.

atmosphere.description: Fog, sky and sun interpolate together. Moving fog without moving sun produces a world lit by nothing — the eye catches it instantly even when it cannot name it.

progress.description: Position on the environment gradient. Must increase monotonically across beats.

hold.description: Milliseconds held after snap. First and last beats MUST be 0 — trapping arrival is the worst first impression and trapping the CTA is the worst last one. All holds are forced to 0 on touch: a swipe that does nothing reads as a broken page.

peak.description: Exactly one beat in the scene may set this. Gets the FOV squeeze, the fog LIFT (not thicken — lifting reads as focus, thickening reads as an effect), the rim bloom and the micro camera shake. If every beat is dramatic, none is.

still.description: Path to the approved storyboard frame. Stage 6 approves this before anything is modelled.
